text: The surgeon incises the junction between the proper rectal fascia and the parietal layer of pelvic fascia, entering the pre-sacral space. The dissection continues downward along the Holy Plane, characterized by loose areolar tissues.

The surgeon continues the posterior mobilization of the rectum until reaching the fourth sacral body, where a thick, tough, opaque fascia is visible. This fascia extends from the periosteum over the vertebral body to the proper fascia of the rectum, approximately three to five centimeters above the anorectal junction, identified as the recto-sacral fascia.

The surgeon incises the recto-sacral fascia, entering the retro-rectal space. At the floor of this space, a dense fascia covering the levator ani muscle and anococcygeal raphe, identified as the Waldeyer's fascia, is visible.
In case number two, the surgeon performs macroscopic dissection of the recto-sacral and valdius fascia. The pre-sacral space is developed along the Holy Plane, revealing loose areolar tissues.

At the level of the third sacral body, the surgeon encounters a thin membranous fibrous tissue that separates the pre-sacral space into superior and inferior compartments, identified as the recto-sacral fascia.
The surgeon cuts the recto-sacral fascia, entering the retro-rectal space. At the floor of this space, the dense Valdez fascia is visible, covering the levator ani muscle and anococcygeal raphe.

In case number three, the surgeon observes Vardy's fascia, which appears more pronounced in patients post-chemoradiation therapy. The presacral space is developed downward to the level of the fourth sacral body, revealing the rectal sacral fascia extending from the fourth sacral body to the proper fascia of the rectum.

The surgeon incises the rectal sacral fascia at the floor of the retro-rectal space, revealing the Valdias fascia. Both the Valdias fascia and rectal sacral fascia appear more pronounced in patients following concurrent chemoradiation therapy.
